text:
*[Bug 19012] foreign::write.foreign() generates incorrect SAS INPUT statement for character variables*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19012>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch
     Ever confirmed|0                           |1
             Status|UNCONFIRMED                 |CONFIRMED

--- Comment #2 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
Well, Troy also added a `URL: ...` entry (easy to miss),
and mentions that the code was changed in svn r5283 - which is correct.
With his details in
`<https://github.com/cornball-ai/rformat-lab/blob/main/foreign_sas_bug.md#notes>`
(me looking up the `svn log -r5283` messages) really very strongly suggests
that the change was an inadvertent mishap.
I have decided to revert that (2008!) change at least for consistency.

I'd still be intersted to know if this just reverted an esthetic inconsistency
(according to Elin's comment #2 it should *not* have lead to problem in SAS
reading) or if it was a strict bug and SAS would *not* read it correctly;
to check that fully, you'd probably also want a version where 
`c = c("1", "2")`  is used such that SAS *could* misinterpret the resulting
files as numeric. 
Anybody out there with easy access to SAS?

text:
*[Bug 19005] as.POSIXlt may produce different attr(,"tzone")[2:3] depending on the order of the calls and values*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19005>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch
     Ever confirmed|0                           |1
             Status|UNCONFIRMED                 |CONFIRMED

--- Comment #2 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
(In reply to Suharto Anggono from comment #1)
> Created attachment 3615
> Patch to datetime.c: set_tz: First, call tzset() while TZ=UTC

Aha, to provide known state in all cases.
Indeed, I see that your patch works in sofar as it fixes this bug and does not
show any new problems in our checks, for both cases
(R-devel on linux configured by default *and* by `--with-internal-tzcode`).

You drop using `R_tzsetwall()` from `src/extra/tzone/localtime.c` in these
changes of your patch
```
-    if(!isUTC && strlen(tz) > 0) set_tz(tz, &tzsi);
-#ifdef USE_INTERNAL_MKTIME
-    else R_tzsetwall(); // to get the system timezone recorded
-#else
-    tzset();
-#endif
+    if(!isUTC) set_tz(tz, &tzsi);
```
which has been suspicious to me, maybe unnecessarily.
Indeed, the `tzset()` defined there (in `localtime.c`) still does call
`R_tzsetwall()` when `getenv("TZ")` is empty (`NULL` in C).
Maybe that _is_ sufficient and indeed your changed `set_tz()` now more closely
resembles `reset_tz()`, probably adding overall consistency.

I'm tempted to accept the patch now, as the bug has been pretty ugly, even
though rare in practice.  Will be waiting a bit for further comments (also off
bugzilla).

text:
*[Bug 19012] foreign::write.foreign() generates incorrect SAS INPUT statement for character variables*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19012>

--- Comment #3 from Elin Waring (<mailto:elin.waring@gmail.com|elin.waring@gmail.com>) ---
Tom (quoted above) and I are working on a full update of writeForeignSAS. There
are a number of issues (and workarounds not needed) because of how SAS has
changed/improved since the code was written.

text:
*[Bug 19012] foreign::write.foreign() generates incorrect SAS INPUT statement for character variables*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19012>

--- Comment #4 from Elin Waring (<mailto:elin.waring@gmail.com|elin.waring@gmail.com>) ---
> c = c("1", "2") is used such that SAS could misinterpret the resulting files as numeric.

Tom says "The way that SAS code is written in the R package there is nothing
for SAS to interpret."  So that means the answer is "no" thus this is not
strictly a bug.
